Meaning/Interpretation: No direct Apollo attestation; plague, purification, and oracular rites forerunners
Evidence/Sources: Early Bronze Age Anatolian and Near Eastern cultic evidence

Meaning/Interpretation: Plague god, purification, protection; Hurrian Aplu means 'son'
Evidence/Sources: Hurrian mythology; Hittite religious texts

Meaning/Interpretation: Tutelary god, protector, plague-averting
Evidence/Sources: Alaksandus Treaty (c. 1280 BCE); Hittite archives

Meaning/Interpretation: Possibly 'protector'; etymology uncertain
Evidence/Sources: Hittite treaties (Alaksandus Treaty); Anatolian linguistics

Meaning/Interpretation: Local guardian and healing deities; Leto (Apollo's mother) has Anatolian roots
Evidence/Sources: Anatolian inscriptions; Luwian hieroglyphic texts

Meaning/Interpretation: Warrior, plague, protection deity; epithet of Reshef
Evidence/Sources: Beth Shan stele; Phoenician inscriptions

Meaning/Interpretation: Bow-wielding hunter deity; possible precursor to Artemis and Apollo
Evidence/Sources: Minoan seals and iconography; Minoan bronze figurines

Meaning/Interpretation: Early theonym; exact function unclear
Evidence/Sources: Linear B tablets (Knossos, Pylos)

Meaning/Interpretation: Transition period; Anatolian, Minoan, and Greek traits merge in regional cults
Evidence/Sources: Collapse of Bronze Age palace culture; archaeological dark age evidence

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Assembly god' (from apella); also interpreted as 'Expeller'
Evidence/Sources: Spartan institutions; Doric month Apellaios; Doric epigraphic evidence

Meaning/Interpretation: Plague god, protector, solar attributes; identified with Apollo
Evidence/Sources: Kition and Idalion inscriptions; bilingual dedications
Syncretized/Related Deities/Figures: Apollo Amyklos, Apollo Hylates, local Cypriot gods

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Destroyer'/'Purifier' (folk etymology); see Plato's Cratylus 405a
Evidence/Sources: Homeric Hymns; Hesiod's Theogony; Plato's Cratylus

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Pythian Apollo'; slayer of the Python; lord of the Delphic oracle; Pythian Games established 582 BCE
Evidence/Sources: Homeric Hymn to Apollo (300–374); Pindar's Pythian Odes; Delphic inscriptions; Aeschylus (Eumenides)

Meaning/Interpretation: Great oracle at Didyma; healing and prophecy through the Branchidae family of priests
Evidence/Sources: Didymaion inscriptions; Herodotus I.46, V.36; Strabo XIV.1.5

Meaning/Interpretation: Oracle at Claros; prophetic vapours; prophecy in verse
Evidence/Sources: Tacitus (Annals II.54); inscriptions at Claros; Iamblichus (De Mysteriis)

Meaning/Interpretation: 'God of the wood'
Evidence/Sources: Kourion sanctuary dedications; Cypriot inscriptions

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Born in Lycia'; archery; Anatolian birth myth of Leto
Evidence/Sources: Homeric epithets (Iliad IV.101); Leto cults; archaeological evidence in Lycia

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Wolf Apollo'; patron of youth and civic order; Lyceum in Athens named after him
Evidence/Sources: Aristotle (Politics VII.1335b); Pausanias I.19.3; Lyceum inscriptions

Meaning/Interpretation: 'God of Alashiya' (ancient Cyprus)
Evidence/Sources: Idalion bilingual dedications; archaeological finds

Meaning/Interpretation: Oracular deity at Gryneion; laurel grove sanctuary
Evidence/Sources: Inscriptions; oracular remains at Gryneion; Pausanias I.21.9

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Of the pastures'; shepherd deity; patron of herdsmen
Evidence/Sources: Arcadian cult sites; Pausanias VIII.38.8

Meaning/Interpretation: Pastoral and military god; associated with the Carneia festival (Sparta's main festival)
Evidence/Sources: Spartan Carneia festival; Pausanias III.13.3–5; archaeological evidence

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Mouse Apollo'; plague-averting; mice sacred to Apollo in the Troad
Evidence/Sources: Homer (Iliad I.37–39); temple at Chryse; coins; Anatolian cult sites

Meaning/Interpretation: Linked to dolphins, sea, purification; Theseus myth; harbours and navigation
Evidence/Sources: Delphic cult; Cretan inscriptions; Pausanias; Homeric Hymn to Apollo (216–386)

Meaning/Interpretation: Northern, shamanic, Scythian traits; the sun god's winter retreat to the Hyperboreans
Evidence/Sources: Herodotus IV.32–35; Pindar (Olympian 3; Pythian 10); Hyperborean myth tradition

Meaning/Interpretation: Protector of roads, doorways, city gates; represented as a conical pillar or baetyl
Evidence/Sources: Aeschylus (Agamemnon 1081); Euripides (Ion 184–218); archaeological pillar dedications

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Averter of evil'; plague-averting; invoked after the Athenian plague (430 BCE); statue by Kalamis/Praxiteles
Evidence/Sources: Pausanias I.3.4; Athenian cult records; Praxiteles' lost statue

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Healer'; healing aspect of Apollo; worshipped at Elis
Evidence/Sources: Pausanias VI.24.6; healing sanctuary inscriptions

Meaning/Interpretation: Soul-traveller; Apollo's raven manifestation; appeared in multiple places simultaneously; author of Arimaspeia
Evidence/Sources: Herodotus IV.13–15; Maximus of Tyre; Pindar fragment

Meaning/Interpretation: Apollo's golden-arrow-riding shaman; healer, purifier, and prophet; travelled the world on Apollo's arrow
Evidence/Sources: Herodotus IV.36; Himerius (Or. 3); Pindar fr. 270 (Bowra)

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Radiant'; solar epithet; the canonical classical form
Evidence/Sources: Delphi inscriptions; Pindar; Aeschylus; Euripides; Plato; Sophocles

Meaning/Interpretation: Solar-warrior; healing; adopted from Greek Apollo with local Etruscan traits
Evidence/Sources: Etruscan mirrors; temple dedications; archaeological finds at Veii; Veii Apollo statue (c. 510 BCE)

Meaning/Interpretation: Fusion of Apollo with Reshef Mikal; healing, solar, apotropaic
Evidence/Sources: Kition inscriptions; Hellenistic dedications; Phoenician-Greek syncretism

Meaning/Interpretation: Fusion of Apollo and Reshef Mikal; healing, solar, apotropaic powers
Evidence/Sources: Greek-Phoenician bilinguals (Kition, Idalion)

Meaning/Interpretation: Sun god, cosmic order; Herodotus identifies Apollo with Horus
Evidence/Sources: Herodotus II.144, 156; Egyptian wall art; syncretic cults; Greco-Egyptian temples

Meaning/Interpretation: Solar syncretism; Apollo fully identified with Helios
Evidence/Sources: Orphic Hymns; Julian's Hymn to Helios; solar iconography

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Who is like God?'; angelic protector, warrior; etymological link to Reshef Mikal
Evidence/Sources: Book of Daniel (Dan 10:13, 21; 12:1)
Syncretized/Related Deities/Figures: Other archangels (Gabriel, Raphael), Canaanite warrior gods

Meaning/Interpretation: Chief angel, defender of Israel, eschatological warrior
Evidence/Sources: 1 Enoch 9–10; Dead Sea Scrolls (1QM); Book of Daniel
Syncretized/Related Deities/Figures: Gabriel, Raphael, Uriel, sons of God

Meaning/Interpretation: Healing springs deity; solar, purification; Emperor Caracalla made a pilgrimage to his shrine (213 CE)
Evidence/Sources: Inscriptions at Aquae Granni (Aachen); altar dedications at Grand (Vosges); CIL XIII
Syncretized/Related Deities/Figures: Celtic Belenus, Sirona (healing goddess), Mars Grannus

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Bright one' or 'brilliant'; solar, healing; linked to Beltane fire festival
Evidence/Sources: Inscriptions across Gaul and Britain; Tertullian (Apol. 24); Ausonius (Comm. Prof. 4)

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Divine youth' (Maponos = 'great son'); music, prophecy, healing
Evidence/Sources: Inscriptions at Corbridge and Ribchester; Hadrian's Wall dedications; Ravenna Cosmography
Syncretized/Related Deities/Figures: Mabon ap Modron (Welsh), Celtic youth deities

Meaning/Interpretation: Healer, plague-averting; first Apollo temple in Rome founded after plague
Evidence/Sources: Livy IV.25.3; Ovid (Fasti); temple records in the Forum Holitorium

Meaning/Interpretation: Patron of Augustus; imperial symbol; laurel, poetry, sun
Evidence/Sources: Augustus' Actium Monument; Virgil's Aeneid VI; Horace's Carmen Saeculare; Palatine sanctuary

Meaning/Interpretation: Solar deity, cosmic ruler; Aurelian merges Apollo and Sol Invictus (274 CE); December 25 dies natalis Solis
Evidence/Sources: Imperial coinage; Mithraea; Aurelian's solar reforms; December 25 festival records

Meaning/Interpretation: Solar, savior, mediator; shared mystery cult features; Nemrut Dağ shows Apollo-Mithras-Hermes handshake
Evidence/Sources: Mithraic iconography; Nemrut Dağ reliefs; Mithraea near Apollo temples

Meaning/Interpretation: Solar, healing, afterlife; Serapis incorporates Apollo's healing and solar roles
Evidence/Sources: Serapis cult; temple dedications; Greco-Egyptian iconography

Meaning/Interpretation: Apostle; debated at Corinth alongside Apollos; Apollo's cult was dominant in Corinth
Evidence/Sources: Letters of Paul (1 Corinthians; Titus); Acts of the Apostles

Meaning/Interpretation: Alexandrian evangelist; companion of Paul; name likely derived from Apollo
Evidence/Sources: Acts 18:24–28; 19:1; 1 Cor 1:12; 3:4–6; 16:12; Titus 3:13; Clement of Rome

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Destroyer'; angel of the abyss; king of locusts; name alludes to Apollo (polemical)
Evidence/Sources: Revelation 9:11; Abaddon in Hebrew Bible (Job 26:6; 28:22; Ps. 88:11); Septuagint

Meaning/Interpretation: 'Destruction'; abyss; angel of death; equated with Apollyon in Revelation
Evidence/Sources: Hebrew Bible; Septuagint; Revelation 9:11

Meaning/Interpretation: Pythagorean wonder-worker; healer; sage; explicitly compared to Christ by Porphyry and Philostratus
Evidence/Sources: Philostratus, Life of Apollonius of Tyana; inscriptions; finds at Tyana and Ephesus

Meaning/Interpretation: Patron of warriors, protector of the Church; converted Apollo sanctuaries into Michaelion churches
Evidence/Sources: Michaelion church at Sosthenion (near Constantinople); hagiographies; Council of Laodicea (363 CE)

Form/Name: St. Michael / Christ as Sol Iustitiae
Meaning/Interpretation: Apollo's solar and healing attributes absorbed into St. Michael (dragon-slayer, radiant healer) and Christ as Sol
Evidence/Sources: Michaelion churches; conversion of Apollo sanctuaries; Christian solar imagery; mosaic iconography

Meaning/Interpretation: Patron of warriors, plague-averting, dragon-slayer; Monte Gargano apparition (490 CE)
Evidence/Sources: Monte Gargano shrine (Italy); Mont Saint-Michel (France); Gregory I (Dialogues)

Meaning/Interpretation: Martyr, dragon-slayer, military saint; iconographically inherits Perseus/Bellerophon/Apollo traditions
Evidence/Sources: Passio Georgii; Golden Legend; Byzantine and Crusader iconography
Syncretized/Related Deities/Figures: St. Michael, Perseus, Bellerophon, local heroes

Meaning/Interpretation: Angel of mercy, sustenance, rain, intercessor; one of the four archangels in Islam
Evidence/Sources: Qur'an 2:98; Hadith literature; Islamic angelology (Ibn Kathir)

Meaning/Interpretation: Solar savior; radiant halo; future Buddha; Apollo's iconographic features (knot, draped robe) transmit to Gandharan Buddhas
Evidence/Sources: Gandharan sculpture: Apollo-knot topknot; radiant halos; Buddhist cave art at Kizil and Bamiyan; Silk Road transmission texts

Meaning/Interpretation: Cosmic sun Buddha (Vairocana = 'he who makes visible by radiance'); infinite light (Amida/Amitabha)
Evidence/Sources: Buddhist texts; cave art (Kizil, Dunhuang); radiant iconography; ushnisha/topknot

Meaning/Interpretation: Music and poetry syncretism; Bragi identified as Norse equivalent of Apollo in modern scholarship
Evidence/Sources: Modern comparative mythology; Snorri Sturluson (Prose Edda); poetic sources

Meaning/Interpretation: Idealized classical Apollo statue (c. 120–140 CE Roman copy); canonical image of ideal male beauty
Evidence/Sources: Vatican Museums; Renaissance art history; Winckelmann (Geschichte der Kunst des Alterthums, 1764); iconographic analysis
